{{神社 |名称= 鴨神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Kamo-jinja (Tamaki) 01.jpg|270px|鴨神社]] |所在地= [[三重県]][[度会郡]][[玉城町]]山神[[小字|字]]岡谷1398 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 40.4 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 36 | 経度秒 = 27.2 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= 石己呂和居命
御前神 |社格= [[式内社]]（小）
{{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}摂社 |創建= |本殿= [[神明造]] |別名= 鴨の谷様 |札所等= |例祭= |神事= [[神嘗祭]] }} '''Kamo Shrine''' (鴨神社) is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂末社}} of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} ({{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}) (Inner Shrine). It is located on the mid-slope of {{ill|Mount Dainichi (Ise)|ja|大日山}}, and is considered the most challenging {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} to reach among the {{ill|125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社}}{{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング}} (2013): p.69. == Overview == {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|鴨神社}} is located at 1398 Okatani, Yamaga, Tamaki Town, Watarai District, Mie PrefectureShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.201. It is one of the 13 auxiliary and subordinate shrines of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Inner Shrine) located in Tamaki Town and ranks 4th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner ShrineUjiyamada City Hall (1929): pp.9–11. The "Kamo" in the shrine name was traditionally believed to refer to an old place name. However, archaeological progress at the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} site has led to suggestions of a connection with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂氏}}, who served as water administratorsShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.199. The shrine grounds cover an area of 15,018 m²Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.202. The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a wooden plank roof, facing south, and is enclosed by a single layer of {{ill|Tamagaki|ja|玉垣}}. It features one {{ill|Shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}}. The sacred object (shintai) is a stoneKaneko (1983): p.59. There is no offertory box. A stream flowing beneath the shrine grounds is said to have never dried up, even during droughtsIse Bunka-sha (2008): p.65. The trees on the grounds display beautiful autumn foliage during late autumn. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are '''Ishikorowake no Mikoto''' (石己呂和居命) and '''Mimae no Kami''' (御前神). Both deities are children of {{ill|Ōminakami no Mikoto|ja|大水上命}} and are considered gods of agriculture and irrigation. Originally, there were separate shrine buildings for each deityUjiyamada City Hall (1929): p.10, but after the shrine was restored by {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} during the Edo period, the deities have been enshrined together in a single structure. == History == According to the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', the shrine was established during the reign of {{ill|Yamato-hime no Mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}Kaneko (1983): p.68, giving it a history as long as the Ise Grand Shrine itselfShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.200. In ancient texts, the shrine is referred to as "Kamo-sha" or "Kamo Jinja," and its reading as "Kamo" is undisputed. It was designated to undergo reconstruction every 20 years, following the tradition of the {{ill|Shikinen Sengū|ja|式年遷宮}} at the Ise Grand Shrine. However, records of reconstruction ceased after an entry from December 8, 1340, which mentions an order to rebuild the shrine using rice tributes from Tajima Province. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), the shrine was restored by Grand Priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} in Yamaga Village, Okatani. {{ill|Mikado Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} later criticized this restoration site as inappropriate in his work ''Ninomiya Kansha Engaku Kō'', proposing that the original site was within Yamaga Village at the remains of the former {{ill|Yahashira Shrine|ja|八柱神社}}, which had been merged in 1908 (Meiji 41)Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.201–202. The current location was established in 1876 (Meiji 9). The shrine building was reconstructed in 1922 (Taishō 11). == Rituals == On the 16th day of the 3rd lunar month, a ritual known as ''Miyatamori Shinji'' (宮田もり神事) is held, which is associated with {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}}Sakurai (1991): p.293. During the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} (June and December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} (October), and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}} (November), rituals are conducted in front of the shrine. For the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭}} (January), {{ill|Kenkoku Kinen no Hi|ja|建国記念祭}} (February 11), {{ill|Fūhi no Sai|ja|風日祈祭}} (May and August), and {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭}} (December 23), remote rituals are performed at the Gojōden of the Ise Grand ShrineShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.200–201. == Access == Near the shrine are the ruins of an ancient icehouse (氷室) associated with the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}. About 2.5 km from Kamo Shrine is the {{ill|Kamoshimo Shrine|ja|鴨下神社}}, which enshrines the same deity and is located within the same Shirota region. ; By Public Transport There are no nearby train stations or bus stops. From {{ill|Tamaru Station|ja|田丸駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}}, it is about a 50-minute walk (4.0 km) southwest, or about 53 minutes (4.3 km) south from {{ill|Sotojōda Station|ja|外城田駅}}. From Yamaga village, a stone marker labeled "Kamo Shrine" leads through a bamboo grove, under a tunnel beneath the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, and along a mountain path to the shrine. The distance from the marker to the shrine is about 1.1 kmIse Bunka-sha (2008): p.63, taking around 20 minutes. There are forestry roads nearby, so caution is advised to avoid getting lost. ; By Car From {{ill|Tamaki Interchange|ja|玉城インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway, it takes about 6 minutes (2.1 km) to reach the stone marker for Kamo Shrine. The mountain path beyond the marker is for pedestrians only, and vehicles cannot pass through. However, there is a parking area near {{ill|Matoyama Park|ja|的山公園}}, which is accessible via a tunnel under the Ise Expressway. This route includes a paved mountain path, reducing the walking distance. {{神社 |名称= 鴨神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Kamo-jinja (Tamaki) 01.jpg|270px|鴨神社]] |所在地= [[三重県]][[度会郡]][[玉城町]]山神[[小字|字]]岡谷1398 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 40.4 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 36 | 経度秒 = 27.2 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= 石己呂和居命
御前神 |社格= [[式内社]]（小）
{{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}摂社 |創建= |本殿= [[神明造]] |別名= 鴨の谷様 |札所等= |例祭= |神事= [[神嘗祭]] }} '''Kamo Shrine''' (鴨神社) is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂末社}} of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} ({{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}) (Inner Shrine). It is located on the mid-slope of {{ill|Mount Dainichi (Ise)|ja|大日山}}, and is considered the most challenging {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} to reach among the {{ill|125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社}}{{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング}} (2013): p.69. == Overview == {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|鴨神社}} is located at 1398 Okatani, Yamaga, Tamaki Town, Watarai District, Mie PrefectureShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.201. It is one of the 13 auxiliary and subordinate shrines of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Inner Shrine) located in Tamaki Town and ranks 4th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner ShrineUjiyamada City Hall (1929): pp.9–11. The "Kamo" in the shrine name was traditionally believed to refer to an old place name. However, archaeological progress at the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} site has led to suggestions of a connection with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂氏}}, who served as water administratorsShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.199. The shrine grounds cover an area of 15,018 m²Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.202. The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a wooden plank roof, facing south, and is enclosed by a single layer of {{ill|Tamagaki|ja|玉垣}}. It features one {{ill|Shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}}. The sacred object (shintai) is a stoneKaneko (1983): p.59. There is no offertory box. A stream flowing beneath the shrine grounds is said to have never dried up, even during droughtsIse Bunka-sha (2008): p.65. The trees on the grounds display beautiful autumn foliage during late autumn. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are '''Ishikorowake no Mikoto''' (石己呂和居命) and '''Mimae no Kami''' (御前神). Both deities are children of {{ill|Ōminakami no Mikoto|ja|大水上命}} and are considered gods of agriculture and irrigation. Originally, there were separate shrine buildings for each deityUjiyamada City Hall (1929): p.10, but after the shrine was restored by {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} during the Edo period, the deities have been enshrined together in a single structure. == History == According to the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', the shrine was established during the reign of {{ill|Yamato-hime no Mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}Kaneko (1983): p.68, giving it a history as long as the Ise Grand Shrine itselfShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.200. In ancient texts, the shrine is referred to as "Kamo-sha" or "Kamo Jinja," and its reading as "Kamo" is undisputed. It was designated to undergo reconstruction every 20 years, following the tradition of the {{ill|Shikinen Sengū|ja|式年遷宮}} at the Ise Grand Shrine. However, records of reconstruction ceased after an entry from December 8, 1340, which mentions an order to rebuild the shrine using rice tributes from Tajima Province. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), the shrine was restored by Grand Priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} in Yamaga Village, Okatani. {{ill|Mikado Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} later criticized this restoration site as inappropriate in his work ''Ninomiya Kansha Engaku Kō'', proposing that the original site was within Yamaga Village at the remains of the former {{ill|Yahashira Shrine|ja|八柱神社}}, which had been merged in 1908 (Meiji 41)Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.201–202. The current location was established in 1876 (Meiji 9). The shrine building was reconstructed in 1922 (Taishō 11). == Rituals == On the 16th day of the 3rd lunar month, a ritual known as ''Miyatamori Shinji'' (宮田もり神事) is held, which is associated with {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}}Sakurai (1991): p.293. During the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} (June and December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} (October), and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}} (November), rituals are conducted in front of the shrine. For the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭}} (January), {{ill|Kenkoku Kinen no Hi|ja|建国記念祭}} (February 11), {{ill|Fūhi no Sai|ja|風日祈祭}} (May and August), and {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭}} (December 23), remote rituals are performed at the Gojōden of the Ise Grand ShrineShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.200–201. == Access == Near the shrine are the ruins of an ancient icehouse (氷室) associated with the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}. About 2.5 km from Kamo Shrine is the {{ill|Kamoshimo Shrine|ja|鴨下神社}}, which enshrines the same deity and is located within the same Shirota region. ; By Public Transport There are no nearby train stations or bus stops. From {{ill|Tamaru Station|ja|田丸駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}}, it is about a 50-minute walk (4.0 km) southwest, or about 53 minutes (4.3 km) south from {{ill|Sotojōda Station|ja|外城田駅}}. From Yamaga village, a stone marker labeled "Kamo Shrine" leads through a bamboo grove, under a tunnel beneath the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, and along a mountain path to the shrine. The distance from the marker to the shrine is about 1.1 kmIse Bunka-sha (2008): p.63, taking around 20 minutes. There are forestry roads nearby, so caution is advised to avoid getting lost. ; By Car From {{ill|Tamaki Interchange|ja|玉城インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway, it takes about 6 minutes (2.1 km) to reach the stone marker for Kamo Shrine. The mountain path beyond the marker is for pedestrians only, and vehicles cannot pass through. However, there is a parking area near {{ill|Matoyama Park|ja|的山公園}}, which is accessible via a tunnel under the Ise Expressway. This route includes a paved mountain path, reducing the walking distance. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), the shrine was restored by Grand Priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} in Yamaga Village, Okatani. {{ill|Mikado Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} later criticized this restoration site as inappropriate in his work ''Ninomiya Kansha Engaku Kō'', proposing that the original site was within Yamaga Village at the remains of the former {{ill|Yahashira Shrine|ja|八柱神社}}, which had been merged in 1908 (Meiji 41)Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.201–202. The current location was established in 1876 (Meiji 9). The shrine building was reconstructed in 1922 (Taishō 11). == Rituals == On the 16th day of the 3rd lunar month, a ritual known as ''Miyatamori Shinji'' (宮田もり神事) is held, which is associated with {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}}Sakurai (1991): p.293. During the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} (June and December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} (October), and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}} (November), rituals are conducted in front of the shrine. For the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭}} (January), {{ill|Kenkoku Kinen no Hi|ja|建国記念祭}} (February 11), {{ill|Fūhi no Sai|ja|風日祈祭}} (May and August), and {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭}} (December 23), remote rituals are performed at the Gojōden of the Ise Grand ShrineShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.200–201. == Access == Near the shrine are the ruins of an ancient icehouse (氷室) associated with the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}. About 2.5 km from Kamo Shrine is the {{ill|Kamoshimo Shrine|ja|鴨下神社}}, which enshrines the same deity and is located within the same Shirota region. ; By Public Transport There are no nearby train stations or bus stops. From {{ill|Tamaru Station|ja|田丸駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}}, it is about a 50-minute walk (4.0 km) southwest, or about 53 minutes (4.3 km) south from {{ill|Sotojōda Station|ja|外城田駅}}. From Yamaga village, a stone marker labeled "Kamo Shrine" leads through a bamboo grove, under a tunnel beneath the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, and along a mountain path to the shrine. The distance from the marker to the shrine is about 1.1 kmIse Bunka-sha (2008): p.63, taking around 20 minutes. There are forestry roads nearby, so caution is advised to avoid getting lost. ; By Car From {{ill|Tamaki Interchange|ja|玉城インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway, it takes about 6 minutes (2.1 km) to reach the stone marker for Kamo Shrine. The mountain path beyond the marker is for pedestrians only, and vehicles cannot pass through. However, there is a parking area near {{ill|Matoyama Park|ja|的山公園}}, which is accessible via a tunnel under the Ise Expressway. This route includes a paved mountain path, reducing the walking distance. {{神社 |名称= 鴨神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Kamo-jinja (Tamaki) 01.jpg|270px|鴨神社]] |所在地= [[三重県]][[度会郡]][[玉城町]]山神[[小字|字]]岡谷1398 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 40.4 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 36 | 経度秒 = 27.2 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= 石己呂和居命
御前神 |社格= [[式内社]]（小）
{{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}摂社 |創建= |本殿= [[神明造]] |別名= 鴨の谷様 |札所等= |例祭= |神事= [[神嘗祭]] }} '''Kamo Shrine''' (鴨神社) is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂末社}} of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} ({{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}) (Inner Shrine). It is located on the mid-slope of {{ill|Mount Dainichi (Ise)|ja|大日山}}, and is considered the most challenging {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} to reach among the {{ill|125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社}}{{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング}} (2013): p.69. == Overview == {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|鴨神社}} is located at 1398 Okatani, Yamaga, Tamaki Town, Watarai District, Mie PrefectureShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.201. It is one of the 13 auxiliary and subordinate shrines of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Inner Shrine) located in Tamaki Town and ranks 4th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner ShrineUjiyamada City Hall (1929): pp.9–11. The "Kamo" in the shrine name was traditionally believed to refer to an old place name. However, archaeological progress at the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} site has led to suggestions of a connection with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂氏}}, who served as water administratorsShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.199. The shrine grounds cover an area of 15,018 m²Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.202. The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a wooden plank roof, facing south, and is enclosed by a single layer of {{ill|Tamagaki|ja|玉垣}}. It features one {{ill|Shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}}. The sacred object (shintai) is a stoneKaneko (1983): p.59. There is no offertory box. A stream flowing beneath the shrine grounds is said to have never dried up, even during droughtsIse Bunka-sha (2008): p.65. The trees on the grounds display beautiful autumn foliage during late autumn. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are '''Ishikorowake no Mikoto''' (石己呂和居命) and '''Mimae no Kami''' (御前神). Both deities are children of {{ill|Ōminakami no Mikoto|ja|大水上命}} and are considered gods of agriculture and irrigation. Originally, there were separate shrine buildings for each deityUjiyamada City Hall (1929): p.10, but after the shrine was restored by {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} during the Edo period, the deities have been enshrined together in a single structure. == History == According to the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', the shrine was established during the reign of {{ill|Yamato-hime no Mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}Kaneko (1983): p.68, giving it a history as long as the Ise Grand Shrine itselfShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.200. In ancient texts, the shrine is referred to as "Kamo-sha" or "Kamo Jinja," and its reading as "Kamo" is undisputed. It was designated to undergo reconstruction every 20 years, following the tradition of the {{ill|Shikinen Sengū|ja|式年遷宮}} at the Ise Grand Shrine. However, records of reconstruction ceased after an entry from December 8, 1340, which mentions an order to rebuild the shrine using rice tributes from Tajima Province. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), the shrine was restored by Grand Priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} in Yamaga Village, Okatani. {{ill|Mikado Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} later criticized this restoration site as inappropriate in his work ''Ninomiya Kansha Engaku Kō'', proposing that the original site was within Yamaga Village at the remains of the former {{ill|Yahashira Shrine|ja|八柱神社}}, which had been merged in 1908 (Meiji 41)Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.201–202. The current location was established in 1876 (Meiji 9). The shrine building was reconstructed in 1922 (Taishō 11). == Rituals == On the 16th day of the 3rd lunar month, a ritual known as ''Miyatamori Shinji'' (宮田もり神事) is held, which is associated with {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}}Sakurai (1991): p.293. During the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} (June and December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} (October), and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}} (November), rituals are conducted in front of the shrine. For the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭}} (January), {{ill|Kenkoku Kinen no Hi|ja|建国記念祭}} (February 11), {{ill|Fūhi no Sai|ja|風日祈祭}} (May and August), and {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭}} (December 23), remote rituals are performed at the Gojōden of the Ise Grand ShrineShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.200–201. == Access == Near the shrine are the ruins of an ancient icehouse (氷室) associated with the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}. About 2.5 km from Kamo Shrine is the {{ill|Kamoshimo Shrine|ja|鴨下神社}}, which enshrines the same deity and is located within the same Shirota region. ; By Public Transport There are no nearby train stations or bus stops. From {{ill|Tamaru Station|ja|田丸駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}}, it is about a 50-minute walk (4.0 km) southwest, or about 53 minutes (4.3 km) south from {{ill|Sotojōda Station|ja|外城田駅}}. From Yamaga village, a stone marker labeled "Kamo Shrine" leads through a bamboo grove, under a tunnel beneath the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, and along a mountain path to the shrine. The distance from the marker to the shrine is about 1.1 kmIse Bunka-sha (2008): p.63, taking around 20 minutes. There are forestry roads nearby, so caution is advised to avoid getting lost. ; By Car From {{ill|Tamaki Interchange|ja|玉城インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway, it takes about 6 minutes (2.1 km) to reach the stone marker for Kamo Shrine. The mountain path beyond the marker is for pedestrians only, and vehicles cannot pass through. However, there is a parking area near {{ill|Matoyama Park|ja|的山公園}}, which is accessible via a tunnel under the Ise Expressway. This route includes a paved mountain path, reducing the walking distance. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58年8月10日、276p. * [[櫻井勝之進]]『伊勢神宮の祖型と展開』[[国書刊行会]]、平成3年11月30日、318p. ISBN 4-336-03296-3 * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[皇學館大学]]出版部、平成2年2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == See Also == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * [[伊勢国の式内社一覧]] == 外部リンク == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area04/area04_05.jpg 鴨神社] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m07/0704.html 鴨神社（かもじんじゃ）] - 財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会 {{125 Shrines of Ise|かもしんしや}} [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]] [[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]][[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[ja:鴨神社 (玉城町)]][[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:玉城町の歴史]] [[Category:玉城町の建築物]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

revision.text:
{{神社 |名称= 鴨神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Kamo-jinja (Tamaki) 01.jpg|270px|鴨神社]] |所在地= [[三重県]][[度会郡]][[玉城町]]山神[[小字|字]]岡谷1398 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 40.4 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 36 | 経度秒 = 27.2 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= 石己呂和居命
御前神 |社格= [[式内社]]（小）
{{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}摂社 |創建= |本殿= [[神明造]] |別名= 鴨の谷様 |札所等= |例祭= |神事= [[神嘗祭]] }} '''Kamo Shrine''' (鴨神社) is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂末社}} of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} ({{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}) (Inner Shrine). It is located on the mid-slope of {{ill|Mount Dainichi (Ise)|ja|大日山}}, and is considered the most challenging {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} to reach among the {{ill|125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社}}{{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング}} (2013): p.69. == Overview == {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|鴨神社}} is located at 1398 Okatani, Yamaga, Tamaki Town, Watarai District, Mie PrefectureShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.201. It is one of the 13 auxiliary and subordinate shrines of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Inner Shrine) located in Tamaki Town and ranks 4th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner ShrineUjiyamada City Hall (1929): pp.9–11. The "Kamo" in the shrine name was traditionally believed to refer to an old place name. However, archaeological progress at the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} site has led to suggestions of a connection with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂氏}}, who served as water administratorsShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.199. The shrine grounds cover an area of 15,018 m²Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.202. The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a wooden plank roof, facing south, and is enclosed by a single layer of {{ill|Tamagaki|ja|玉垣}}. It features one {{ill|Shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}}. The sacred object (shintai) is a stoneKaneko (1983): p.59. There is no offertory box. A stream flowing beneath the shrine grounds is said to have never dried up, even during droughtsIse Bunka-sha (2008): p.65. The trees on the grounds display beautiful autumn foliage during late autumn. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are '''Ishikorowake no Mikoto''' (石己呂和居命) and '''Mimae no Kami''' (御前神). Both deities are children of {{ill|Ōminakami no Mikoto|ja|大水上命}} and are considered gods of agriculture and irrigation. Originally, there were separate shrine buildings for each deityUjiyamada City Hall (1929): p.10, but after the shrine was restored by {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} during the Edo period, the deities have been enshrined together in a single structure. == History == According to the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', the shrine was established during the reign of {{ill|Yamato-hime no Mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}Kaneko (1983): p.68, giving it a history as long as the Ise Grand Shrine itselfShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.200. In ancient texts, the shrine is referred to as "Kamo-sha" or "Kamo Jinja," and its reading as "Kamo" is undisputed. It was designated to undergo reconstruction every 20 years, following the tradition of the {{ill|Shikinen Sengū|ja|式年遷宮}} at the Ise Grand Shrine. However, records of reconstruction ceased after an entry from December 8, 1340, which mentions an order to rebuild the shrine using rice tributes from Tajima Province. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), the shrine was restored by Grand Priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} in Yamaga Village, Okatani. {{ill|Mikado Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} later criticized this restoration site as inappropriate in his work ''Ninomiya Kansha Engaku Kō'', proposing that the original site was within Yamaga Village at the remains of the former {{ill|Yahashira Shrine|ja|八柱神社}}, which had been merged in 1908 (Meiji 41)Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.201–202. The current location was established in 1876 (Meiji 9). The shrine building was reconstructed in 1922 (Taishō 11). == Rituals == On the 16th day of the 3rd lunar month, a ritual known as ''Miyatamori Shinji'' (宮田もり神事) is held, which is associated with {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}}Sakurai (1991): p.293. During the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} (June and December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} (October), and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}} (November), rituals are conducted in front of the shrine. For the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭}} (January), {{ill|Kenkoku Kinen no Hi|ja|建国記念祭}} (February 11), {{ill|Fūhi no Sai|ja|風日祈祭}} (May and August), and {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭}} (December 23), remote rituals are performed at the Gojōden of the Ise Grand ShrineShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.200–201. == Access == Near the shrine are the ruins of an ancient icehouse (氷室) associated with the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}. About 2.5 km from Kamo Shrine is the {{ill|Kamoshimo Shrine|ja|鴨下神社}}, which enshrines the same deity and is located within the same Shirota region. ; By Public Transport There are no nearby train stations or bus stops. From {{ill|Tamaru Station|ja|田丸駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}}, it is about a 50-minute walk (4.0 km) southwest, or about 53 minutes (4.3 km) south from {{ill|Sotojōda Station|ja|外城田駅}}. From Yamaga village, a stone marker labeled "Kamo Shrine" leads through a bamboo grove, under a tunnel beneath the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, and along a mountain path to the shrine. The distance from the marker to the shrine is about 1.1 kmIse Bunka-sha (2008): p.63, taking around 20 minutes. There are forestry roads nearby, so caution is advised to avoid getting lost. ; By Car From {{ill|Tamaki Interchange|ja|玉城インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway, it takes about 6 minutes (2.1 km) to reach the stone marker for Kamo Shrine. The mountain path beyond the marker is for pedestrians only, and vehicles cannot pass through. However, there is a parking area near {{ill|Matoyama Park|ja|的山公園}}, which is accessible via a tunnel under the Ise Expressway. This route includes a paved mountain path, reducing the walking distance. [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[ja:鴨神社]] == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}} == 参考文献 == * [[伊勢文化舎]] 編『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、伊勢文化舎、平成20年12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4年1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013年7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58年8月10日、276p. * [[櫻井勝之進]]『伊勢神宮の祖型と展開』[[国書刊行会]]、平成3年11月30日、318p. ISBN 4-336-03296-3 * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[皇學館大学]]出版部、平成2年2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == See Also == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * [[伊勢国の式内社一覧]] == External Links == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area04/area04_05.jpg 鴨神社] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m07/0704.html 鴨神社（かもじんじゃ）] - 財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会 {{125 Shrines of Ise|かもしんしや}} [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]] [[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]][[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[ja:鴨神社 (玉城町)]][[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:玉城町の歴史]] [[Category:玉城町の建築物]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

{{神社 |名称= 鴨神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Kamo-jinja (Tamaki) 01.jpg|270px|鴨神社]] |所在地= [[三重県]][[度会郡]][[玉城町]]山神[[小字|字]]岡谷1398 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 40.4 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 36 | 経度秒 = 27.2 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= 石己呂和居命
御前神 |社格= [[式内社]]（小）
{{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}摂社 |創建= |本殿= [[神明造]] |別名= 鴨の谷様 |札所等= |例祭= |神事= [[神嘗祭]] }} '''Kamo Shrine''' (鴨神社) is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂末社}} of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} ({{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}) (Inner Shrine). It is located on the mid-slope of {{ill|Mount Dainichi (Ise)|ja|大日山}}, and is considered the most challenging {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} to reach among the {{ill|125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社}}{{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング}} (2013): p.69. == Overview == {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|鴨神社}} is located at 1398 Okatani, Yamaga, Tamaki Town, Watarai District, Mie PrefectureShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.201. It is one of the 13 auxiliary and subordinate shrines of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Inner Shrine) located in Tamaki Town and ranks 4th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner ShrineUjiyamada City Hall (1929): pp.9–11. The "Kamo" in the shrine name was traditionally believed to refer to an old place name. However, archaeological progress at the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} site has led to suggestions of a connection with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂氏}}, who served as water administratorsShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.199. The shrine grounds cover an area of 15,018 m²Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.202. The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a wooden plank roof, facing south, and is enclosed by a single layer of {{ill|Tamagaki|ja|玉垣}}. It features one {{ill|Shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}}. The sacred object (shintai) is a stoneKaneko (1983): p.59. There is no offertory box. A stream flowing beneath the shrine grounds is said to have never dried up, even during droughtsIse Bunka-sha (2008): p.65. The trees on the grounds display beautiful autumn foliage during late autumn. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are '''Ishikorowake no Mikoto''' (石己呂和居命) and '''Mimae no Kami''' (御前神). Both deities are children of {{ill|Ōminakami no Mikoto|ja|大水上命}} and are considered gods of agriculture and irrigation. Originally, there were separate shrine buildings for each deityUjiyamada City Hall (1929): p.10, but after the shrine was restored by {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} during the Edo period, the deities have been enshrined together in a single structure. == History == According to the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', the shrine was established during the reign of {{ill|Yamato-hime no Mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}Kaneko (1983): p.68, giving it a history as long as the Ise Grand Shrine itselfShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.200. In ancient texts, the shrine is referred to as "Kamo-sha" or "Kamo Jinja," and its reading as "Kamo" is undisputed. It was designated to undergo reconstruction every 20 years, following the tradition of the {{ill|Shikinen Sengū|ja|式年遷宮}} at the Ise Grand Shrine. However, records of reconstruction ceased after an entry from December 8, 1340, which mentions an order to rebuild the shrine using rice tributes from Tajima Province. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), the shrine was restored by Grand Priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} in Yamaga Village, Okatani. {{ill|Mikado Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} later criticized this restoration site as inappropriate in his work ''Ninomiya Kansha Engaku Kō'', proposing that the original site was within Yamaga Village at the remains of the former {{ill|Yahashira Shrine|ja|八柱神社}}, which had been merged in 1908 (Meiji 41)Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.201–202. The current location was established in 1876 (Meiji 9). The shrine building was reconstructed in 1922 (Taishō 11). == Rituals == On the 16th day of the 3rd lunar month, a ritual known as ''Miyatamori Shinji'' (宮田もり神事) is held, which is associated with {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}}Sakurai (1991): p.293. During the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} (June and December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} (October), and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}} (November), rituals are conducted in front of the shrine. For the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭}} (January), {{ill|Kenkoku Kinen no Hi|ja|建国記念祭}} (February 11), {{ill|Fūhi no Sai|ja|風日祈祭}} (May and August), and {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭}} (December 23), remote rituals are performed at the Gojōden of the Ise Grand ShrineShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.200–201. == Access == Near the shrine are the ruins of an ancient icehouse (氷室) associated with the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}. About 2.5 km from Kamo Shrine is the {{ill|Kamoshimo Shrine|ja|鴨下神社}}, which enshrines the same deity and is located within the same Shirota region. ; By Public Transport There are no nearby train stations or bus stops. From {{ill|Tamaru Station|ja|田丸駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}}, it is about a 50-minute walk (4.0 km) southwest, or about 53 minutes (4.3 km) south from {{ill|Sotojōda Station|ja|外城田駅}}. From Yamaga village, a stone marker labeled "Kamo Shrine" leads through a bamboo grove, under a tunnel beneath the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, and along a mountain path to the shrine. The distance from the marker to the shrine is about 1.1 kmIse Bunka-sha (2008): p.63, taking around 20 minutes. There are forestry roads nearby, so caution is advised to avoid getting lost. ; By Car From {{ill|Tamaki Interchange|ja|玉城インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway, it takes about 6 minutes (2.1 km) to reach the stone marker for Kamo Shrine. The mountain path beyond the marker is for pedestrians only, and vehicles cannot pass through. However, there is a parking area near {{ill|Matoyama Park|ja|的山公園}}, which is accessible via a tunnel under the Ise Expressway. This route includes a paved mountain path, reducing the walking distance. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), the shrine was restored by Grand Priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} in Yamaga Village, Okatani. {{ill|Mikado Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} later criticized this restoration site as inappropriate in his work ''Ninomiya Kansha Engaku Kō'', proposing that the original site was within Yamaga Village at the remains of the former {{ill|Yahashira Shrine|ja|八柱神社}}, which had been merged in 1908 (Meiji 41)Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.201–202. The current location was established in 1876 (Meiji 9). The shrine building was reconstructed in 1922 (Taishō 11). == Rituals == On the 16th day of the 3rd lunar month, a ritual known as ''Miyatamori Shinji'' (宮田もり神事) is held, which is associated with {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}}Sakurai (1991): p.293. During the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} (June and December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} (October), and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}} (November), rituals are conducted in front of the shrine. For the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭}} (January), {{ill|Kenkoku Kinen no Hi|ja|建国記念祭}} (February 11), {{ill|Fūhi no Sai|ja|風日祈祭}} (May and August), and {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭}} (December 23), remote rituals are performed at the Gojōden of the Ise Grand ShrineShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.200–201. == Access == Near the shrine are the ruins of an ancient icehouse (氷室) associated with the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}. About 2.5 km from Kamo Shrine is the {{ill|Kamoshimo Shrine|ja|鴨下神社}}, which enshrines the same deity and is located within the same Shirota region. ; By Public Transport There are no nearby train stations or bus stops. From {{ill|Tamaru Station|ja|田丸駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}}, it is about a 50-minute walk (4.0 km) southwest, or about 53 minutes (4.3 km) south from {{ill|Sotojōda Station|ja|外城田駅}}. From Yamaga village, a stone marker labeled "Kamo Shrine" leads through a bamboo grove, under a tunnel beneath the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, and along a mountain path to the shrine. The distance from the marker to the shrine is about 1.1 kmIse Bunka-sha (2008): p.63, taking around 20 minutes. There are forestry roads nearby, so caution is advised to avoid getting lost. ; By Car From {{ill|Tamaki Interchange|ja|玉城インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway, it takes about 6 minutes (2.1 km) to reach the stone marker for Kamo Shrine. The mountain path beyond the marker is for pedestrians only, and vehicles cannot pass through. However, there is a parking area near {{ill|Matoyama Park|ja|的山公園}}, which is accessible via a tunnel under the Ise Expressway. This route includes a paved mountain path, reducing the walking distance. {{神社 |名称= 鴨神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Kamo-jinja (Tamaki) 01.jpg|270px|鴨神社]] |所在地= [[三重県]][[度会郡]][[玉城町]]山神[[小字|字]]岡谷1398 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 40.4 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 36 | 経度秒 = 27.2 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= 石己呂和居命
御前神 |社格= [[式内社]]（小）
{{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}摂社 |創建= |本殿= [[神明造]] |別名= 鴨の谷様 |札所等= |例祭= |神事= [[神嘗祭]] }} '''Kamo Shrine''' (鴨神社) is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂末社}} of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} ({{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}) (Inner Shrine). It is located on the mid-slope of {{ill|Mount Dainichi (Ise)|ja|大日山}}, and is considered the most challenging {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} to reach among the {{ill|125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社}}{{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング}} (2013): p.69. == Overview == {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|鴨神社}} is located at 1398 Okatani, Yamaga, Tamaki Town, Watarai District, Mie PrefectureShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.201. It is one of the 13 auxiliary and subordinate shrines of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Inner Shrine) located in Tamaki Town and ranks 4th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner ShrineUjiyamada City Hall (1929): pp.9–11. The "Kamo" in the shrine name was traditionally believed to refer to an old place name. However, archaeological progress at the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} site has led to suggestions of a connection with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂氏}}, who served as water administratorsShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.199. The shrine grounds cover an area of 15,018 m²Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.202. The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a wooden plank roof, facing south, and is enclosed by a single layer of {{ill|Tamagaki|ja|玉垣}}. It features one {{ill|Shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}}. The sacred object (shintai) is a stoneKaneko (1983): p.59. There is no offertory box. A stream flowing beneath the shrine grounds is said to have never dried up, even during droughtsIse Bunka-sha (2008): p.65. The trees on the grounds display beautiful autumn foliage during late autumn. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are '''Ishikorowake no Mikoto''' (石己呂和居命) and '''Mimae no Kami''' (御前神). Both deities are children of {{ill|Ōminakami no Mikoto|ja|大水上命}} and are considered gods of agriculture and irrigation. Originally, there were separate shrine buildings for each deityUjiyamada City Hall (1929): p.10, but after the shrine was restored by {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} during the Edo period, the deities have been enshrined together in a single structure. == History == According to the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', the shrine was established during the reign of {{ill|Yamato-hime no Mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}Kaneko (1983): p.68, giving it a history as long as the Ise Grand Shrine itselfShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.200. In ancient texts, the shrine is referred to as "Kamo-sha" or "Kamo Jinja," and its reading as "Kamo" is undisputed. It was designated to undergo reconstruction every 20 years, following the tradition of the {{ill|Shikinen Sengū|ja|式年遷宮}} at the Ise Grand Shrine. However, records of reconstruction ceased after an entry from December 8, 1340, which mentions an order to rebuild the shrine using rice tributes from Tajima Province. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), the shrine was restored by Grand Priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} in Yamaga Village, Okatani. {{ill|Mikado Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} later criticized this restoration site as inappropriate in his work ''Ninomiya Kansha Engaku Kō'', proposing that the original site was within Yamaga Village at the remains of the former {{ill|Yahashira Shrine|ja|八柱神社}}, which had been merged in 1908 (Meiji 41)Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.201–202. The current location was established in 1876 (Meiji 9). The shrine building was reconstructed in 1922 (Taishō 11). == Rituals == On the 16th day of the 3rd lunar month, a ritual known as ''Miyatamori Shinji'' (宮田もり神事) is held, which is associated with {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}}Sakurai (1991): p.293. During the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} (June and December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} (October), and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}} (November), rituals are conducted in front of the shrine. For the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭}} (January), {{ill|Kenkoku Kinen no Hi|ja|建国記念祭}} (February 11), {{ill|Fūhi no Sai|ja|風日祈祭}} (May and August), and {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭}} (December 23), remote rituals are performed at the Gojōden of the Ise Grand ShrineShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.200–201. == Access == Near the shrine are the ruins of an ancient icehouse (氷室) associated with the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}. About 2.5 km from Kamo Shrine is the {{ill|Kamoshimo Shrine|ja|鴨下神社}}, which enshrines the same deity and is located within the same Shirota region. ; By Public Transport There are no nearby train stations or bus stops. From {{ill|Tamaru Station|ja|田丸駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}}, it is about a 50-minute walk (4.0 km) southwest, or about 53 minutes (4.3 km) south from {{ill|Sotojōda Station|ja|外城田駅}}. From Yamaga village, a stone marker labeled "Kamo Shrine" leads through a bamboo grove, under a tunnel beneath the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, and along a mountain path to the shrine. The distance from the marker to the shrine is about 1.1 kmIse Bunka-sha (2008): p.63, taking around 20 minutes. There are forestry roads nearby, so caution is advised to avoid getting lost. ; By Car From {{ill|Tamaki Interchange|ja|玉城インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway, it takes about 6 minutes (2.1 km) to reach the stone marker for Kamo Shrine. The mountain path beyond the marker is for pedestrians only, and vehicles cannot pass through. However, there is a parking area near {{ill|Matoyama Park|ja|的山公園}}, which is accessible via a tunnel under the Ise Expressway. This route includes a paved mountain path, reducing the walking distance. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == See Also == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * [[伊勢国の式内社一覧]] == External Links == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area04/area04_05.jpg 鴨神社] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m07/0704.html 鴨神社（かもじんじゃ）] - 財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会 {{125 Shrines of Ise|かもしんしや}} [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]] [[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]] [[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[ja:鴨神社 (玉城町)]][[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:玉城町の歴史]] [[Category:玉城町の建築物]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

[[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[ja:鴨神社]] == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}} == 参考文献 == * [[伊勢文化舎]] 編『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、伊勢文化舎、平成20年12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4年1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013年7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58年8月10日、276p. * [[櫻井勝之進]]『伊勢神宮の祖型と展開』[[国書刊行会]]、平成3年11月30日、318p. ISBN 4-336-03296-3 * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[皇學館大学]]出版部、平成2年2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == See Also == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * [[伊勢国の式内社一覧]] == External Links == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area04/area04_05.jpg 鴨神社] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m07/0704.html 鴨神社（かもじんじゃ）] - 財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会 {{125 Shrines of Ise|かもしんしや}} [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]] [[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]] [[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[ja:鴨神社 (玉城町)]][[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:玉城町の歴史]] [[Category:玉城町の建築物]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

{{神社 |名称= 鴨神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Kamo-jinja (Tamaki) 01.jpg|270px|鴨神社]] |所在地= [[三重県]][[度会郡]][[玉城町]]山神[[小字|字]]岡谷1398 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 40.4 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 36 | 経度秒 = 27.2 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= 石己呂和居命
御前神 |社格= [[式内社]]（小）
{{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}摂社 |創建= |本殿= [[神明造]] |別名= 鴨の谷様 |札所等= |例祭= |神事= [[神嘗祭]] }} '''Kamo Shrine''' (鴨神社) is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂末社}} of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} ({{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}) (Inner Shrine). It is located on the mid-slope of {{ill|Mount Dainichi (Ise)|ja|大日山}}, and is considered the most challenging {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} to reach among the {{ill|125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社}}{{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング}} (2013): p.69. == Overview == {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|鴨神社}} is located at 1398 Okatani, Yamaga, Tamaki Town, Watarai District, Mie PrefectureShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.201. It is one of the 13 auxiliary and subordinate shrines of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Inner Shrine) located in Tamaki Town and ranks 4th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner ShrineUjiyamada City Hall (1929): pp.9–11. The "Kamo" in the shrine name was traditionally believed to refer to an old place name. However, archaeological progress at the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} site has led to suggestions of a connection with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂氏}}, who served as water administratorsShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.199. The shrine grounds cover an area of 15,018 m²Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.202. The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a wooden plank roof, facing south, and is enclosed by a single layer of {{ill|Tamagaki|ja|玉垣}}. It features one {{ill|Shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}}. The sacred object (shintai) is a stoneKaneko (1983): p.59. There is no offertory box. A stream flowing beneath the shrine grounds is said to have never dried up, even during droughtsIse Bunka-sha (2008): p.65. The trees on the grounds display beautiful autumn foliage during late autumn. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are '''Ishikorowake no Mikoto''' (石己呂和居命) and '''Mimae no Kami''' (御前神). Both deities are children of {{ill|Ōminakami no Mikoto|ja|大水上命}} and are considered gods of agriculture and irrigation. Originally, there were separate shrine buildings for each deityUjiyamada City Hall (1929): p.10, but after the shrine was restored by {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} during the Edo period, the deities have been enshrined together in a single structure. == History == According to the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', the shrine was established during the reign of {{ill|Yamato-hime no Mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}Kaneko (1983): p.68, giving it a history as long as the Ise Grand Shrine itselfShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.200. In ancient texts, the shrine is referred to as "Kamo-sha" or "Kamo Jinja," and its reading as "Kamo" is undisputed. It was designated to undergo reconstruction every 20 years, following the tradition of the {{ill|Shikinen Sengū|ja|式年遷宮}} at the Ise Grand Shrine. However, records of reconstruction ceased after an entry from December 8, 1340, which mentions an order to rebuild the shrine using rice tributes from Tajima Province. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), the shrine was restored by Grand Priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} in Yamaga Village, Okatani. {{ill|Mikado Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} later criticized this restoration site as inappropriate in his work ''Ninomiya Kansha Engaku Kō'', proposing that the original site was within Yamaga Village at the remains of the former {{ill|Yahashira Shrine|ja|八柱神社}}, which had been merged in 1908 (Meiji 41)Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.201–202. The current location was established in 1876 (Meiji 9). The shrine building was reconstructed in 1922 (Taishō 11). == Rituals == On the 16th day of the 3rd lunar month, a ritual known as ''Miyatamori Shinji'' (宮田もり神事) is held, which is associated with {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}}Sakurai (1991): p.293. During the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} (June and December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} (October), and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}} (November), rituals are conducted in front of the shrine. For the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭}} (January), {{ill|Kenkoku Kinen no Hi|ja|建国記念祭}} (February 11), {{ill|Fūhi no Sai|ja|風日祈祭}} (May and August), and {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭}} (December 23), remote rituals are performed at the Gojōden of the Ise Grand ShrineShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.200–201. == Access == Near the shrine are the ruins of an ancient icehouse (氷室) associated with the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}. About 2.5 km from Kamo Shrine is the {{ill|Kamoshimo Shrine|ja|鴨下神社}}, which enshrines the same deity and is located within the same Shirota region. ; By Public Transport There are no nearby train stations or bus stops. From {{ill|Tamaru Station|ja|田丸駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}}, it is about a 50-minute walk (4.0 km) southwest, or about 53 minutes (4.3 km) south from {{ill|Sotojōda Station|ja|外城田駅}}. From Yamaga village, a stone marker labeled "Kamo Shrine" leads through a bamboo grove, under a tunnel beneath the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, and along a mountain path to the shrine. The distance from the marker to the shrine is about 1.1 kmIse Bunka-sha (2008): p.63, taking around 20 minutes. There are forestry roads nearby, so caution is advised to avoid getting lost. ; By Car From {{ill|Tamaki Interchange|ja|玉城インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway, it takes about 6 minutes (2.1 km) to reach the stone marker for Kamo Shrine. The mountain path beyond the marker is for pedestrians only, and vehicles cannot pass through. However, there is a parking area near {{ill|Matoyama Park|ja|的山公園}}, which is accessible via a tunnel under the Ise Expressway. This route includes a paved mountain path, reducing the walking distance. [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[ja:鴨神社]] == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}} == 参考文献 == * [[伊勢文化舎]] 編『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、伊勢文化舎、平成20年12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4年1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013年7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58年8月10日、276p. * [[櫻井勝之進]]『伊勢神宮の祖型と展開』[[国書刊行会]]、平成3年11月30日、318p. ISBN 4-336-03296-3 * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[皇學館大学]]出版部、平成2年2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == See Also == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * [[伊勢国の式内社一覧]] == External Links == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area04/area04_05.jpg 鴨神社] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m07/0704.html 鴨神社（かもじんじゃ）] - 財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会 {{125 Shrines of Ise|かもしんしや}} [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]] [[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]] [[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[ja:鴨神社 (玉城町)]][[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:玉城町の歴史]] [[Category:玉城町の建築物]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

御前神 |社格= [[式内社]]（小）
{{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}摂社 |創建= |本殿= [[神明造]] |別名= 鴨の谷様 |札所等= |例祭= |神事= [[神嘗祭]] }} '''Kamo Shrine''' (鴨神社) is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂末社}} of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} ({{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}) (Inner Shrine). It is located on the mid-slope of {{ill|Mount Dainichi (Ise)|ja|大日山}}, and is considered the most challenging {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} to reach among the {{ill|125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社}}{{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング}} (2013): p.69. == Overview == {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|鴨神社}} is located at 1398 Okatani, Yamaga, Tamaki Town, Watarai District, Mie PrefectureShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.201. It is one of the 13 auxiliary and subordinate shrines of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Inner Shrine) located in Tamaki Town and ranks 4th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner ShrineUjiyamada City Hall (1929): pp.9–11. The "Kamo" in the shrine name was traditionally believed to refer to an old place name. However, archaeological progress at the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} site has led to suggestions of a connection with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂氏}}, who served as water administratorsShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.199. The shrine grounds cover an area of 15,018 m²Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.202. The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a wooden plank roof, facing south, and is enclosed by a single layer of {{ill|Tamagaki|ja|玉垣}}. It features one {{ill|Shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}}. The sacred object (shintai) is a stoneKaneko (1983): p.59. There is no offertory box. A stream flowing beneath the shrine grounds is said to have never dried up, even during droughtsIse Bunka-sha (2008): p.65. The trees on the grounds display beautiful autumn foliage during late autumn. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are '''Ishikorowake no Mikoto''' (石己呂和居命) and '''Mimae no Kami''' (御前神). Both deities are children of {{ill|Ōminakami no Mikoto|ja|大水上命}} and are considered gods of agriculture and irrigation. Originally, there were separate shrine buildings for each deityUjiyamada City Hall (1929): p.10, but after the shrine was restored by {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} during the Edo period, the deities have been enshrined together in a single structure. == History == According to the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', the shrine was established during the reign of {{ill|Yamato-hime no Mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}Kaneko (1983): p.68, giving it a history as long as the Ise Grand Shrine itselfShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.200. In ancient texts, the shrine is referred to as "Kamo-sha" or "Kamo Jinja," and its reading as "Kamo" is undisputed. It was designated to undergo reconstruction every 20 years, following the tradition of the {{ill|Shikinen Sengū|ja|式年遷宮}} at the Ise Grand Shrine. However, records of reconstruction ceased after an entry from December 8, 1340, which mentions an order to rebuild the shrine using rice tributes from Tajima Province. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), the shrine was restored by Grand Priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} in Yamaga Village, Okatani. {{ill|Mikado Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} later criticized this restoration site as inappropriate in his work ''Ninomiya Kansha Engaku Kō'', proposing that the original site was within Yamaga Village at the remains of the former {{ill|Yahashira Shrine|ja|八柱神社}}, which had been merged in 1908 (Meiji 41)Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.201–202. The current location was established in 1876 (Meiji 9). The shrine building was reconstructed in 1922 (Taishō 11). == Rituals == On the 16th day of the 3rd lunar month, a ritual known as ''Miyatamori Shinji'' (宮田もり神事) is held, which is associated with {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}}Sakurai (1991): p.293. During the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} (June and December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} (October), and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}} (November), rituals are conducted in front of the shrine. For the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭}} (January), {{ill|Kenkoku Kinen no Hi|ja|建国記念祭}} (February 11), {{ill|Fūhi no Sai|ja|風日祈祭}} (May and August), and {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭}} (December 23), remote rituals are performed at the Gojōden of the Ise Grand ShrineShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.200–201. == Access == Near the shrine are the ruins of an ancient icehouse (氷室) associated with the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}. About 2.5 km from Kamo Shrine is the {{ill|Kamoshimo Shrine|ja|鴨下神社}}, which enshrines the same deity and is located within the same Shirota region. ; By Public Transport There are no nearby train stations or bus stops. From {{ill|Tamaru Station|ja|田丸駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}}, it is about a 50-minute walk (4.0 km) southwest, or about 53 minutes (4.3 km) south from {{ill|Sotojōda Station|ja|外城田駅}}. From Yamaga village, a stone marker labeled "Kamo Shrine" leads through a bamboo grove, under a tunnel beneath the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, and along a mountain path to the shrine. The distance from the marker to the shrine is about 1.1 kmIse Bunka-sha (2008): p.63, taking around 20 minutes. There are forestry roads nearby, so caution is advised to avoid getting lost. ; By Car From {{ill|Tamaki Interchange|ja|玉城インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway, it takes about 6 minutes (2.1 km) to reach the stone marker for Kamo Shrine. The mountain path beyond the marker is for pedestrians only, and vehicles cannot pass through. However, there is a parking area near {{ill|Matoyama Park|ja|的山公園}}, which is accessible via a tunnel under the Ise Expressway. This route includes a paved mountain path, reducing the walking distance. During the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} (June and December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} (October), and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}} (November), rituals are conducted in front of the shrine. For the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭}} (January), {{ill|Kenkoku Kinen no Hi|ja|建国記念祭}} (February 11), {{ill|Fūhi no Sai|ja|風日祈祭}} (May and August), and {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭}} (December 23), remote rituals are performed at the Gojōden of the Ise Grand ShrineShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.200–201. == Access == Near the shrine are the ruins of an ancient icehouse (氷室) associated with the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}. About 2.5 km from Kamo Shrine is the {{ill|Kamoshimo Shrine|ja|鴨下神社}}, which enshrines the same deity and is located within the same Shirota region. ; By Public Transport There are no nearby train stations or bus stops. From {{ill|Tamaru Station|ja|田丸駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}}, it is about a 50-minute walk (4.0 km) southwest, or about 53 minutes (4.3 km) south from {{ill|Sotojōda Station|ja|外城田駅}}. From Yamaga village, a stone marker labeled "Kamo Shrine" leads through a bamboo grove, under a tunnel beneath the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, and along a mountain path to the shrine. The distance from the marker to the shrine is about 1.1 kmIse Bunka-sha (2008): p.63, taking around 20 minutes. There are forestry roads nearby, so caution is advised to avoid getting lost. ; By Car From {{ill|Tamaki Interchange|ja|玉城インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway, it takes about 6 minutes (2.1 km) to reach the stone marker for Kamo Shrine. The mountain path beyond the marker is for pedestrians only, and vehicles cannot pass through. However, there is a parking area near {{ill|Matoyama Park|ja|的山公園}}, which is accessible via a tunnel under the Ise Expressway. This route includes a paved mountain path, reducing the walking distance. {{神社 |名称= 鴨神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Kamo-jinja (Tamaki) 01.jpg|270px|鴨神社]] |所在地= [[三重県]][[度会郡]][[玉城町]]山神[[小字|字]]岡谷1398 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 40.4 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 36 | 経度秒 = 27.2 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= 石己呂和居命
御前神 |社格= [[式内社]]（小）
{{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}摂社 |創建= |本殿= [[神明造]] |別名= 鴨の谷様 |札所等= |例祭= |神事= [[神嘗祭]] }} '''Kamo Shrine''' (鴨神社) is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂末社}} of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} ({{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}) (Inner Shrine). It is located on the mid-slope of {{ill|Mount Dainichi (Ise)|ja|大日山}}, and is considered the most challenging {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} to reach among the {{ill|125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社}}{{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング}} (2013): p.69. == Overview == {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|鴨神社}} is located at 1398 Okatani, Yamaga, Tamaki Town, Watarai District, Mie PrefectureShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.201. It is one of the 13 auxiliary and subordinate shrines of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Inner Shrine) located in Tamaki Town and ranks 4th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner ShrineUjiyamada City Hall (1929): pp.9–11. The "Kamo" in the shrine name was traditionally believed to refer to an old place name. However, archaeological progress at the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} site has led to suggestions of a connection with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂氏}}, who served as water administratorsShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.199. The shrine grounds cover an area of 15,018 m²Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.202. The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a wooden plank roof, facing south, and is enclosed by a single layer of {{ill|Tamagaki|ja|玉垣}}. It features one {{ill|Shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}}. The sacred object (shintai) is a stoneKaneko (1983): p.59. There is no offertory box. A stream flowing beneath the shrine grounds is said to have never dried up, even during droughtsIse Bunka-sha (2008): p.65. The trees on the grounds display beautiful autumn foliage during late autumn. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are '''Ishikorowake no Mikoto''' (石己呂和居命) and '''Mimae no Kami''' (御前神). Both deities are children of {{ill|Ōminakami no Mikoto|ja|大水上命}} and are considered gods of agriculture and irrigation. Originally, there were separate shrine buildings for each deityUjiyamada City Hall (1929): p.10, but after the shrine was restored by {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} during the Edo period, the deities have been enshrined together in a single structure. == History == According to the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', the shrine was established during the reign of {{ill|Yamato-hime no Mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}Kaneko (1983): p.68, giving it a history as long as the Ise Grand Shrine itselfShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.200. In ancient texts, the shrine is referred to as "Kamo-sha" or "Kamo Jinja," and its reading as "Kamo" is undisputed. It was designated to undergo reconstruction every 20 years, following the tradition of the {{ill|Shikinen Sengū|ja|式年遷宮}} at the Ise Grand Shrine. However, records of reconstruction ceased after an entry from December 8, 1340, which mentions an order to rebuild the shrine using rice tributes from Tajima Province. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), the shrine was restored by Grand Priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} in Yamaga Village, Okatani. {{ill|Mikado Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} later criticized this restoration site as inappropriate in his work ''Ninomiya Kansha Engaku Kō'', proposing that the original site was within Yamaga Village at the remains of the former {{ill|Yahashira Shrine|ja|八柱神社}}, which had been merged in 1908 (Meiji 41)Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.201–202. The current location was established in 1876 (Meiji 9). The shrine building was reconstructed in 1922 (Taishō 11). == Rituals == On the 16th day of the 3rd lunar month, a ritual known as ''Miyatamori Shinji'' (宮田もり神事) is held, which is associated with {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}}Sakurai (1991): p.293. During the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} (June and December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} (October), and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}} (November), rituals are conducted in front of the shrine. For the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭}} (January), {{ill|Kenkoku Kinen no Hi|ja|建国記念祭}} (February 11), {{ill|Fūhi no Sai|ja|風日祈祭}} (May and August), and {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭}} (December 23), remote rituals are performed at the Gojōden of the Ise Grand ShrineShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.200–201. == Access == Near the shrine are the ruins of an ancient icehouse (氷室) associated with the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}. About 2.5 km from Kamo Shrine is the {{ill|Kamoshimo Shrine|ja|鴨下神社}}, which enshrines the same deity and is located within the same Shirota region. ; By Public Transport There are no nearby train stations or bus stops. From {{ill|Tamaru Station|ja|田丸駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}}, it is about a 50-minute walk (4.0 km) southwest, or about 53 minutes (4.3 km) south from {{ill|Sotojōda Station|ja|外城田駅}}. From Yamaga village, a stone marker labeled "Kamo Shrine" leads through a bamboo grove, under a tunnel beneath the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, and along a mountain path to the shrine. The distance from the marker to the shrine is about 1.1 kmIse Bunka-sha (2008): p.63, taking around 20 minutes. There are forestry roads nearby, so caution is advised to avoid getting lost. ; By Car From {{ill|Tamaki Interchange|ja|玉城インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway, it takes about 6 minutes (2.1 km) to reach the stone marker for Kamo Shrine. The mountain path beyond the marker is for pedestrians only, and vehicles cannot pass through. However, there is a parking area near {{ill|Matoyama Park|ja|的山公園}}, which is accessible via a tunnel under the Ise Expressway. This route includes a paved mountain path, reducing the walking distance. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == See Also == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧}} * [[伊勢国の式内社一覧]] == External Links == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area04/area04_05.jpg 鴨神社] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m07/0704.html 鴨神社（かもじんじゃ）] - 財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会 {{125 Shrines of Ise|かもしんしや}} [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]] [[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]] [[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[ja:鴨神社 (玉城町)]][[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:玉城町の歴史]] [[Category:玉城町の建築物]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

revision.text:
{{神社 |名称= 鴨神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Kamo-jinja (Tamaki) 01.jpg|270px|鴨神社]] |所在地= [[三重県]][[度会郡]][[玉城町]]山神[[小字|字]]岡谷1398 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 40.4 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 36 | 経度秒 = 27.2 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= 石己呂和居命
御前神 |社格= [[式内社]]（小）
{{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}摂社 |創建= |本殿= [[神明造]] |別名= 鴨の谷様 |札所等= |例祭= |神事= [[神嘗祭]] }} '''Kamo Shrine''' (鴨神社) is a {{ill|sessha|ja|摂末社}} of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} ({{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}) (Inner Shrine). It is located on the mid-slope of {{ill|Mount Dainichi (Ise)|ja|大日山}}, and is considered the most challenging {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} to reach among the {{ill|125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社}}{{ill|Gakken Publishing|ja|学研パブリッシング}} (2013): p.69. == Overview == {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|鴨神社}} is located at 1398 Okatani, Yamaga, Tamaki Town, Watarai District, Mie PrefectureShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.201. It is one of the 13 auxiliary and subordinate shrines of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Inner Shrine) located in Tamaki Town and ranks 4th among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner ShrineUjiyamada City Hall (1929): pp.9–11. The "Kamo" in the shrine name was traditionally believed to refer to an old place name. However, archaeological progress at the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} site has led to suggestions of a connection with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂氏}}, who served as water administratorsShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.199. The shrine grounds cover an area of 15,018 m²Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.202. The shrine building is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a wooden plank roof, facing south, and is enclosed by a single layer of {{ill|Tamagaki|ja|玉垣}}. It features one {{ill|Shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}}. The sacred object (shintai) is a stoneKaneko (1983): p.59. There is no offertory box. A stream flowing beneath the shrine grounds is said to have never dried up, even during droughtsIse Bunka-sha (2008): p.65. The trees on the grounds display beautiful autumn foliage during late autumn. == Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities are '''Ishikorowake no Mikoto''' (石己呂和居命) and '''Mimae no Kami''' (御前神). Both deities are children of {{ill|Ōminakami no Mikoto|ja|大水上命}} and are considered gods of agriculture and irrigation. Originally, there were separate shrine buildings for each deityUjiyamada City Hall (1929): p.10, but after the shrine was restored by {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} during the Edo period, the deities have been enshrined together in a single structure. == History == According to the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', the shrine was established during the reign of {{ill|Yamato-hime no Mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}Kaneko (1983): p.68, giving it a history as long as the Ise Grand Shrine itselfShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): p.200. In ancient texts, the shrine is referred to as "Kamo-sha" or "Kamo Jinja," and its reading as "Kamo" is undisputed. It was designated to undergo reconstruction every 20 years, following the tradition of the {{ill|Shikinen Sengū|ja|式年遷宮}} at the Ise Grand Shrine. However, records of reconstruction ceased after an entry from December 8, 1340, which mentions an order to rebuild the shrine using rice tributes from Tajima Province. In 1663 (Kanbun 3), the shrine was restored by Grand Priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} in Yamaga Village, Okatani. {{ill|Mikado Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} later criticized this restoration site as inappropriate in his work ''Ninomiya Kansha Engaku Kō'', proposing that the original site was within Yamaga Village at the remains of the former {{ill|Yahashira Shrine|ja|八柱神社}}, which had been merged in 1908 (Meiji 41)Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.201–202. The current location was established in 1876 (Meiji 9). The shrine building was reconstructed in 1922 (Taishō 11). == Rituals == On the 16th day of the 3rd lunar month, a ritual known as ''Miyatamori Shinji'' (宮田もり神事) is held, which is associated with {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}}Sakurai (1991): p.293. During the {{ill|Kinen-sai|ja|祈年祭}} (February), {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} (June and December), {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} (October), and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}} (November), rituals are conducted in front of the shrine. For the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}} (January 1), {{ill|Genshi-sai|ja|元始祭}} (January), {{ill|Kenkoku Kinen no Hi|ja|建国記念祭}} (February 11), {{ill|Fūhi no Sai|ja|風日祈祭}} (May and August), and {{ill|Tenchō-sai|ja|天長祭}} (December 23), remote rituals are performed at the Gojōden of the Ise Grand ShrineShikinaisha Kenkyūkai (1990): pp.200–201. == Access == Near the shrine are the ruins of an ancient icehouse (氷室) associated with the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}}. About 2.5 km from Kamo Shrine is the {{ill|Kamoshimo Shrine|ja|鴨下神社}}, which enshrines the same deity and is located within the same Shirota region. ; By Public Transport There are no nearby train stations or bus stops. From {{ill|Tamaru Station|ja|田丸駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}}, it is about a 50-minute walk (4.0 km) southwest, or about 53 minutes (4.3 km) south from {{ill|Sotojōda Station|ja|外城田駅}}. From Yamaga village, a stone marker labeled "Kamo Shrine" leads through a bamboo grove, under a tunnel beneath the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, and along a mountain path to the shrine. The distance from the marker to the shrine is about 1.1 kmIse Bunka-sha (2008): p.63, taking around 20 minutes. There are forestry roads nearby, so caution is advised to avoid getting lost. ; By Car From {{ill|Tamaki Interchange|ja|玉城インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway, it takes about 6 minutes (2.1 km) to reach the stone marker for Kamo Shrine. The mountain path beyond the marker is for pedestrians only, and vehicles cannot pass through. However, there is a parking area near {{ill|Matoyama Park|ja|的山公園}}, which is accessible via a tunnel under the Ise Expressway. This route includes a paved mountain path, reducing the walking distance. [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[ja:鴨神社]] == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}} == 参考文献 == * [[伊勢文化舎]] 編『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、伊勢文化舎、平成20年12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4年1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013年7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58年8月10日、276p. * [[櫻井勝之進]]『伊勢神宮の祖型と展開』[[国書刊行会]]、平成3年11月30日、318p. ISBN 4-336-03296-3 * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[Kogakkan University]]出版部、平成2年2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 == See Also == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧}} * [[伊勢国の式内社一覧]] == External Links == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area04/area04_05.jpg 鴨神社] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m07/0704.html 鴨神社（かもじんじゃ）] - 財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会 {{125 Shrines of Ise|かもしんしや}} [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]] [[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]] [[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[ja:鴨神社 (玉城町)]][[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:玉城町の歴史]] [[Category:玉城町の建築物]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo Shrine (Ise)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]
